cloud_providers: Azure: South Africa North (Johannesburg) + South Africa West (Cape Town)
GCP: africa-south1 full region (Johannesburg, launched Jan 2024)
key_regulations: National AI Policy Framework (draft, published Aug 2024)
dc_pipeline: 6 upcoming; Teraco JB5/JB7, Vantage JHB1, Equinix JN1 Phase 2, Africa Data Centres CPT expansion — ~185 MW additional capacity by 2026
ai_compute_availability: Strong. Teraco JB4 (50MW, liquid-to-chip cooling, AI-ready), Equinix JN1 (20MW, liquid cooling, high-density), Africa Data Centres JHB/CPT (GPU-colocatable), Vantage JHB campus (80MW planned). NVIDIA–Cassava partnership active. Microsoft $300M investment through 2027.
cloud_maturity: Highest in Africa. All four major hyperscalers operate full regions in South Africa. AWS af-south-1 (Cape Town) since 2020; Azure South Africa North (Johannesburg) since 2019; GCP africa-south1 (Johannesburg) launched January 2024; Oracle OCI af-johannesburg-1 live.
connectivity_role: Southern Africa's primary internet hub. Nine submarine cables with 400+ Tbps combined capacity. Four community-run IXPs (JINX, CINX, DINX, NMBINX) with 200+ peering networks. JINX dark fibre upgrades completed 2024.
power_reliability: Markedly improved. Load shedding effectively ended March 2024; Eskom unplanned outages down 5,506 MW YoY. Energy Availability Factor up 12.57%. Enterprise DCs run on N+2 UPS with diesel backup; minimal impact on colocation uptime.
data_residency_constraint: POPIA mandates data localisation or adequacy/consent safeguards for cross-border transfers. All four hyperscalers offer local regions meeting POPIA residency requirements.
founder_insight: South Africa is the only African country with all four major hyperscaler full regions, making it the lowest-risk base for deploying inference workloads under POPIA. Teraco's carrier-neutral JB1–JB4 campus with liquid-to-chip AI cooling and Equinix JN1 now provide genuine GPU colocation without importing hardware. The March 2024 end of load shedding removes the last major operational risk — enterprise DCs that survived Stage 4–6 blackouts with diesel have robust backup, but grid stability now means lower OpEx and simpler SLA commitments.
ai_data_governance_posture: POPIA is enforced and mature (GDPR-equivalent); the August 2024 National AI Policy Framework sets nine pillars including ethical AI and POPIA alignment. No dedicated AI Act yet, but regulatory direction is clear and industry-friendly.
ai_compute_policy_commitment: Microsoft pledged USD 300M for cloud and AI infrastructure (2025–2027). NVIDIA–Cassava partnership for AI-ready DCs across Africa. GCP africa-south1 launched January 2024. National AI Policy Framework prioritises compute infrastructure as a strategic pillar.
cross_border_ai_alignment: POPIA adequacy framework allows EU and UK data flows under existing arrangements. South Africa endorsed the AU Continental AI Strategy (June 2024). Active in SADC digital economy frameworks.

cloud_providers: AWS: Nearest full region: af-south-1 (Cape Town); no Kenya region
Azure: East Africa Cloud Region announced (Kenya, G42 geothermal partnership, ~100MW, targeting 2026–2027)
GCP: Nearest full region: africa-south1 (Johannesburg); no Kenya region confirmed
Oracle: Oracle Cloud Region Kenya announced (hosted at iXAfrica, timeline TBC)
Huawei: PoP presence; no dedicated Kenya cloud region
dc_pipeline: 9 upcoming; iXAfrica NBO1 (22.5MW hyperscale AI-ready), Microsoft/G42 Naivasha campus (100MW geothermal, 2026–2027), Africa Data Centres Nairobi expansion — 150+ MW planned
ai_compute_availability: Emerging-Strong. iXAfrica NBOX1 is East Africa's first explicitly AI-ready hyperscale DC: 22.5MW capacity, 50kW/rack via free-air cooling, carrier-neutral, KIXP PoP on-site (Feb 2025). Oracle Cloud Region Kenya to be hosted at iXAfrica. Africa Data Centres Nairobi supports GPU-colocatable high-density racks. 90%+ renewable energy provides a green compute advantage.
cloud_maturity: Transitional. Oracle has announced a Cloud Region at iXAfrica (Kenya). Microsoft/Azure committed to an East Africa Region (geothermal-powered, ~2026). AWS has no Kenya-specific infrastructure beyond CDN. GCP serves from Johannesburg. Kenya is on the cusp of becoming a second African hyperscaler hub by 2027.
connectivity_role: East Africa's primary internet hub. Eight submarine cables at Mombasa. KIXP (123 peers, 2.5 Tbps) expanded with new PoP at iXAfrica (Feb 2025). Low-latency terrestrial fibre to Uganda, Tanzania, Rwanda via national backbone.
power_reliability: Good and improving. 90% renewable mix (geothermal 47% baseload provides 24/7 stability). Outages typically <2h/day in Nairobi; geothermal baseload limits severity. Kenya's power is Africa's cleanest and most consistent for DC operations after South Africa's 2024 grid recovery.
data_residency_constraint: Data Protection Act 2019 governs personal data transfers; cross-border transfers allowed to adequate jurisdictions or under contractual safeguards. National AI Strategy 2025–2030 sets new data governance obligations. No hyperscaler local region yet means full residency depends on iXAfrica/Africa Data Centres colocation.
founder_insight: Kenya is East Africa's inference hub-in-waiting — iXAfrica's 50kW/rack AI-ready campus is operational now, Oracle's Cloud Region is coming, and Microsoft/Azure's geothermal-powered East Africa Region targets 2026. With 90%+ renewable energy, GPU colocation here carries a genuine ESG argument that resonates with EU enterprise buyers. The M-Pesa developer ecosystem (100,000+ Daraja API integrations, 60,000+ integrations) represents a uniquely deep fintech distribution layer for AI financial products across East and Southern Africa.
ai_data_governance_posture: Data Protection Act 2019 is comprehensive and enforced (ODPC established). National AI Strategy 2025–2030 launched March 2025 — first in Sub-Saharan Africa — establishing AI governance framework and regulatory roadmap. No dedicated AI Act yet; KEBS Code of Practice expected within 1–2 years.
ai_compute_policy_commitment: Microsoft/G42 $1B commitment for geothermal AI campus (May 2024). Oracle Cloud Region at iXAfrica announced. Kenya National AI Strategy prioritises compute and data infrastructure. Startup Act 2022 (enacted July 2024) provides tax breaks and credit access for AI startups.
cross_border_ai_alignment: DPA 2019 allows transfers to adequate jurisdictions; ODPC published cross-border transfer guidelines. Kenya endorsed AU Continental AI Strategy. Active EAC digital economy harmonisation. Strong alignment with EU AI Act principles in national AI strategy.

dc_operators: Rogers Capital Technology Services (Ebene, La Tour Koenig — Tier IV)
key_regulations: National AI Strategy 2018 (updated in Government Programme 2024–2029)
FSC Guidelines on Responsible Use of AI (Sept 2025)
dc_pipeline: T4 submarine cable (replacing SAFE, 1000x capacity, targeting 2027); Mauritius Telecom MUR 20bn infrastructure investment 2024–2027; additional smart city DC capacity planned for Ebene CyberCity
ai_compute_availability: Moderate — Rogers Capital Tier IV and Africa Data Centres offer carrier-neutral colocation with GPU-ready power densities; Cloudflare and AWS edge PoPs reduce CDN latency; no hyperscaler full region but cloud on-ramp via South Africa Johannesburg region is sub-150ms
cloud_maturity: Edge PoP (Cloudflare Port Louis, AWS CloudFront); hyperscaler full regions in Johannesburg reachable at ~100ms
connectivity_role: Indian Ocean crossroads — 6 submarine cables connecting Africa, Asia and Europe; positioned as intercontinental corridor linking sub-Saharan Africa to Asia; Mauritius Telecom investing to become regional digital hub
power_reliability: Good — CEB-managed grid with 18.2% renewables (2024); urban reliability high; data centers have independent UPS/generator backup; 60% renewable target by 2030 in progress
data_residency_constraint: Data Protection Act 2017 (GDPR-equivalent) allows cross-border transfers to adequate countries; well-established compliance environment; BPO sector has mature data governance frameworks
est_rtt_to_europe_ms: ~120–160ms (via SAFE/T3 to South Africa then onward to Europe; direct Indian Ocean routes to Middle East ~80ms)
founder_insight: Mauritius is the most regulation-ready AI jurisdiction in sub-Saharan island Africa — its GDPR-aligned Data Protection Act, published AI Strategy, and Tier IV Rogers Capital DC give enterprise AI builders a credible compliance and infrastructure base. The island's 6 submarine cables and Cloudflare/AWS edge PoPs make it viable for low-latency inference serving the Indian Ocean corridor and East African enterprise market. The main constraint is market size (~1.25M population) and the absence of a hyperscaler full region, meaning GPU-intensive training must still run in Johannesburg or Europe.
ai_data_governance_posture: GDPR-aligned DPA 2017 actively enforced; FSC AI guidelines for financial services (Sept 2025); government AI unit and ethical AI policy in Digital Transformation Strategy 2025–2029
ai_compute_policy_commitment: Government Programme 2024–2029 explicitly commits to AI development; EDB Mauritius actively marketing island as AI/cloud hub; MUR 20bn telecom investment includes cloud/DC infrastructure
cross_border_ai_alignment: Strong — GDPR-equivalent framework; EU adequacy discussions ongoing; active SADC and AU digital framework participation

cloud_providers: AWS: Wavelength Zone Casablanca (Orange partnership, GA Jan 2025) — 5G edge; no full region
Azure: Nearest full region: West Europe (Netherlands); CDN PoP; no Morocco region
GCP: Maroc Telecom regional data hub partnership announced; no dedicated region
Oracle: Two Cloud Regions: Casablanca (live 2025, hosted at N+ONE) + Settat (planned)
key_regulations: Law 09-08 on Personal Data Protection (CNDP oversight)
Bill for National AI Governance Agency (proposed 2024)
dc_pipeline: Morocco leads Africa in upcoming DC capacity share (35–55% of continent pipeline). N+ONE Nouaceur Tier III (operational late 2024), Orange Tech Casablanca 1.5MW (2025), Naver/Iozera 500MW Tetouan megacampus (NVIDIA partnership, renewable-powered, 2026+) — largest planned AI DC project in Africa by MW
ai_compute_availability: Emerging. Oracle Cloud Region Casablanca (N+ONE, live 2025) provides the most capable AI cloud service with OCI Generative AI and GPU infrastructure. AWS Wavelength Zone in Orange Casablanca DC supports 5G edge AI. Iozera/Naver 500MW campus with NVIDIA at Tetouan will transform Morocco's position when delivered (2026+). Current GPU colocation limited outside Oracle OCI.
cloud_maturity: Accelerating. Oracle OCI has a live Cloud Region in Casablanca (N+ONE, 2025) — the first full hyperscaler region in francophone/Maghreb Africa. AWS Wavelength Zone in Casablanca (Orange, Jan 2025). GCP-Maroc Telecom regional hub announced. Azure serves from West Europe (~50–60ms). Morocco is positioned to become North Africa's cloud hub given Marseille proximity and Oracle's early commitment.
connectivity_role: North Africa's emerging internet hub with Europe's lowest latency from any African country after Egypt (~50–60ms RTT to Marseille). WACS and ACE provide West African and European connectivity; EllaLink adds direct Brazil link; Medusa (2025) adds Mediterranean ring redundancy. CAS-IX (Casablanca IXP, 2019) is the sole IXP — a gap being addressed. AWS Wavelength in Orange DC is the anchor for low-latency edge services.
power_reliability: Reliable. No significant scheduled outages. Grid powered ~60% coal, 27% renewables (2024) with strong ONEE management. 3 GW North-South HVDC project contracted (Nov 2024) improves renewable integration. Enterprise DCs in Casablanca operate without material power interruption.
data_residency_constraint: Law 09-08 (CNDP) requires authorisation for personal data transfers outside Morocco unless to adequate countries. Decree 2-24-921 (Oct 2024) mandates sovereign cloud requirements for critical organisations. Oracle OCI Casablanca satisfies in-country residency. Digital X.0 Framework Law will codify AI data governance when passed.
founder_insight: Morocco offers the lowest latency to Europe of any North/West African AI deployment (~50–60ms Casablanca to Marseille), with Oracle OCI already live in Casablanca and reliable power. The Naver/Iozera 500MW megacampus with NVIDIA at Tetouan is the most ambitious AI infrastructure project on the continent — if delivered, it positions Morocco as Africa's first GPU hyperscale destination. The regulatory environment is predictable (CNDP, Law 09-08) with Digital X.0 modernising the framework. The gap is ecosystem maturity: at $95M in 2024 VC funding, Morocco trails Kenya and Nigeria significantly — B2C AI applications need regional scale from Day 1.
ai_data_governance_posture: Law 09-08 and CNDP provide a functional data protection regime, though less mature than POPIA or PDPL. Digital X.0 Framework Law (draft 2025) will establish AI deployment rules, data sovereignty requirements, and interoperability standards. CNDP-Ministry agreement for national LLM platform signed Sept 2025. Sovereign cloud decree (Oct 2024) applies to critical infrastructure operators.
ai_compute_policy_commitment: Government signed protocol for Data and AI Excellence Center in Casablanca-Settat (July 2025). Oracle opened R&D centre employing 1,000 AI professionals (June 2025). $142M allocated for startup ecosystem (Dec 2025) with AI focus. Naver 500MW AI campus (NVIDIA partnership) under planning. National renewable energy programme targets 52% by 2030, directly supporting DC decarbonisation.
cross_border_ai_alignment: Law 09-08 allows transfers to adequate countries (EU adequacy via Arab region framework). Decree 2-24-921 aligns with EU NIS2 and data sovereignty norms. Morocco has an active EU Association Agreement covering digital trade. Strong alignment with French AI governance frameworks (Onepoint partnership). Member of AMU and Arab League AI coordination.

cloud_providers: AWS: Local Zone Lagos (Jan 2023) + Direct Connect at Rack Centre; no full region
Azure: Nearest full region: South Africa North; Lagos CDN edge PoP
GCP: Nearest full region: africa-south1 (Johannesburg); CDN edge PoP Lagos
Oracle: No dedicated region; nearest: af-johannesburg-1
Huawei: Nigeria Cloud Region (Lagos, launched Dec 2024) — GPU-enabled racks for AI/fintech
key_regulations: General Application and Implementation Directive (GAID) 2025 (effective Sept 2025)
dc_pipeline: 16 upcoming; Africa Data Centres 10MW Lagos hall, Digital Realty Abuja Phase 2, 21st Century Technologies hyperscale — ~340 MW additional capacity by 2028
ai_compute_availability: Emerging. Equinix LG1/LG2 (Lagos, 14.5MW+, carrier-neutral, GPU-colocatable via Schneider/NVIDIA AI pods up to 80kW/cabinet). Rack Centre (14.5MW, AWS Direct Connect anchor). Huawei Nigeria Cloud Region launched Dec 2024 with GPU racks. AWS Local Zone Lagos for low-latency edge. No full hyperscaler region yet — a significant gap for training workloads.
cloud_maturity: Edge-tier. AWS operates a Local Zone in Lagos (Jan 2023) and Direct Connect at Rack Centre. Huawei launched a dedicated Nigeria GPU cloud region (Dec 2024). Azure and GCP serve Nigeria from South Africa North and africa-south1 with CDN edge PoPs in Lagos. No AWS/Azure/GCP full region; announced Lagos AWS Region timeline unconfirmed as of early 2026.
connectivity_role: West Africa's primary internet transit hub. Eight submarine cables; Equiano and 2Africa (landed Akwa Ibom 2024) added major capacity. IXPN exceeded 1 Tbps in Q1 2025 and hosted AfPIF 2025. Google Global Cache and Microsoft Connected Cache expanded significantly in Lagos 2024–2025.
power_reliability: Severe constraint. Grid delivers average ~4 hours/day; 12 nationwide collapses in 2024. Enterprise DCs operate on 100% diesel/gas generator backup (N+2), adding 20–35% to OpEx. Power is the single largest operational friction for AI inference deployment.
data_residency_constraint: NDPA 2023 restricts cross-border personal data transfers without NDPC authorisation or adequacy determination. Huawei's local cloud region addresses in-country residency. GAID 2025 introduces stricter obligations for large-scale AI data processors.
founder_insight: Nigeria has Africa's largest consumer market and a $6B tech funding base (2019–2024), but power infrastructure is the critical blocker for AI inference at scale — every Lagos DC runs on diesel at significant cost premium. The Huawei Nigeria Cloud Region (Dec 2024) is the first GPU-capable cloud service with genuine in-country residency, filling the gap until a major hyperscaler full region arrives. NDPA 2023 and NAIS 2024 provide a clear and modern regulatory framework — compliance overhead is lower than founders expect, especially for B2B AI products in fintech and agritech.
ai_data_governance_posture: NDPA 2023 is Nigeria's comprehensive data protection law (GDPR-comparable). NDPC established as independent regulator. GAID 2025 specifically addresses AI/ML data processing. National AI Strategy 2024 is adopted with five pillars; dedicated AI Act under parliamentary review.
ai_compute_policy_commitment: NAIS 2024 designates compute infrastructure as Pillar 1. Lagos state committed ₦31B innovation fund (Aug 2025). GITEX Nigeria 2025 attracted $6B tech investment commitments. IXPN expanding DNS and cache infrastructure nationally through 2025.
cross_border_ai_alignment: NDPA allows cross-border transfers to NDPC-approved countries or under contractual safeguards. Nigeria endorsing AU Continental AI Strategy. Active ECOWAS digital economy harmonisation discussions.

cloud_providers: AWS: CloudFront edge location Cairo (May 2024); Global Accelerator PoP (Aug 2024); no full region
Azure: Nearest full region: UAE North (Dubai); CDN PoP Cairo
GCP: Nearest full region: me-central1 (Doha) or africa-south1; edge PoP only
Oracle: No dedicated Egypt region announced
Huawei: Huawei Cloud Cairo region (launched 2024, USD 300M) — GPU-enabled, Arabic-language AI services
dc_operators: Telecom Egypt (Smart Village RDH, Tier III)
Khazna Data Centers (hyperscale planned, $250M, Maadi Tech Park)
dc_pipeline: 7 upcoming; Khazna hyperscale ($250M, Maadi Tech Park), GPX Cairo 2 expansion, Telecom Egypt new national DC, UAE-Egypt 1 GW DC MoU (Dec 2024) — ~180 MW in 2025–2030 pipeline
ai_compute_availability: Early-stage. Telecom Egypt Smart Village RDH (Tier III, connected to 14 cables) and GPX Cairo 2 are the most capable current facilities. Huawei Cloud Cairo (2024) provides the only in-country GPU/AI cloud service. AWS/Azure/GCP serve Egypt from edge PoPs only. Khazna's $250M hyperscale DC will be the first genuine hyperscale facility when delivered.
cloud_maturity: Edge-only for major Western hyperscalers. AWS has CloudFront edge and Global Accelerator PoP in Cairo (2024) but no full region. Huawei launched a dedicated Cairo cloud region in 2024 with GPU and Arabic AI capabilities. Oracle has no Egypt region. Azure and GCP serve from UAE/Gulf. Egypt's cable transit dominance has not yet translated into Western hyperscaler region investment.
connectivity_role: World's most strategic submarine cable transit corridor. 15 active cables across Mediterranean and Red Sea coasts; SEA-ME-WE-6 added July 2025. EG-IX (AMS-IX partnership, first open access IXP at Smart Village) and CAIX operational. Telecom Egypt RDH connected to 14+ cable systems — unique transoceanic peering point between Europe, Asia, and Africa.
power_reliability: Constrained. Planned outages of 2–3 hours/day in 2024 due to gas supply shortages. Enterprise DCs use generator backup but rolling cuts raise OpEx. Egypt targets 10 GW renewable capacity by 2025 which should improve reliability by 2026–2027.
data_residency_constraint: PDPL 2020 restricts personal data transfers abroad unless destination has adequate protection or specific conditions are met. Government cloud strategy requires ministries' data to remain in Egypt. Huawei Cloud Cairo satisfies in-country residency. Hyperscaler edge-only presence creates a gap for regulated industries.
founder_insight: Egypt's extraordinary submarine cable position — 15 active cables with the world's densest transoceanic transit chokepoint — gives it the lowest latency to Europe of any African country (~30–50ms to Marseille/Frankfurt). For AI inference serving European and MENA markets simultaneously, Egypt is structurally superior to South Africa in connectivity terms. The gap is compute: Huawei Cloud Cairo is the only in-country GPU cloud today, with Khazna's $250M hyperscale DC in the pipeline. PDPL 2020 is enacted but enforcement maturity lags POPIA — founders targeting government or regulated sectors should plan for local data storage from day one.
ai_data_governance_posture: PDPL No. 151/2020 is enacted with executive regulations in force. National AI Strategy 2025–2030 (2nd edition) is a presidential-level commitment with USD 42.7B economic target by 2030. Government operating its own national cloud DC. Strong top-down AI governance intent, though PDPL enforcement maturity is still developing.
ai_compute_policy_commitment: National AI Strategy 2025–2030 targets 7.7% ICT-to-GDP and 30,000 AI specialists annually. UAE-Egypt MoU (Dec 2024) for 1 GW data center capacity. Huawei $300M Cairo cloud region live. SEA-ME-WE-6 landed July 2025.
cross_border_ai_alignment: PDPL allows transfers to countries on MCIT adequacy list or under standard contractual clauses. Egypt is the most active AU/Africa member in ITU AI governance forums. National AI Strategy references OECD AI Principles and G20 AI alignment. Strategic partnership with UAE on AI and data center infrastructure.

dc_operators: Equinix (AB1, AB2, Abidjan, Tier III, carrier-neutral)
Raxio CIV1 (Abidjan, Tier III, 3MW, 800 racks, opened Sep 2024)
key_regulations: Law No. 2013-450 on Personal Data Protection (ARTCI oversight)
National AI and Data Governance Strategy SNIA 2030 (adopted Dec 2024)
ARTCI data protection permanent exchange framework
dc_pipeline: ST Digital Grand-Bassam Tier III (~4,000sqm, AI-designed infra, construction 2025)
ai_compute_availability: Raxio CIV1 (3MW, 800 racks, carrier-neutral) and Equinix AB1/AB2 available; no GPU cloud region
cloud_maturity: Edge PoP only — no hyperscaler region; Equinix fabric enables cloud on-ramp routing
connectivity_role: West Africa Francophone hub — 5 submarine cable landings at Abidjan; CIVIX IXP
power_reliability: Moderate — 2-4h outages daily on average; improving but distribution inconsistent outside Abidjan
data_residency_constraint: Law No. 2013-450 restricts cross-border personal data transfer without ARTCI approval
founder_insight: Abidjan is West Africa's most connected Francophone hub with 5 submarine cable landings and a formally adopted national AI strategy (SNIA 2030, Dec 2024). Raxio CIV1 and Equinix AB1/AB2 provide carrier-neutral colocation that enables hybrid edge deployments today without hyperscaler dependency. No hyperscaler region exists yet, so latency-sensitive inference must route via Lagos or Europe, adding 20–40ms overhead and creating a clear market gap for a regional cloud PoP.
ai_data_governance_posture: Data Protection Law since 2013 with active ARTCI enforcement; SNIA 2030 adopted Dec 2024 — strongest governance framework in Francophone West Africa
ai_compute_policy_commitment: SNIA 2030 explicitly targets domestic AI infrastructure investment, local hosting tax incentives, and national AI skills development
cross_border_ai_alignment: ECOWAS digital frameworks member; ARTCI participates in African data protection authority networks; SNIA 2030 includes international governance pillar

cloud_providers: AWS (Wavelength Zone via Orange/MTN, parent region eu-west-3 Paris)
dc_pipeline: Africa Data Centres Accra (10MW phase 1, DFC-backed, under construction); PAIX Accra expansion planned
ai_compute_availability: Limited but growing — Equinix AC1 and Digital Realty ACR2 GPU-colocatable; ADC Accra will unlock higher-density AI workloads
cloud_maturity: Wavelength Zone (AWS via Orange/MTN 5G edge); no full hyperscaler region
connectivity_role: West Africa coastal hub
power_reliability: Moderate — historic 'dumsor' load-shedding; major DCs on independent power
data_residency_constraint: Data Protection Act 2012 requires consent for cross-border transfer; no blanket localisation mandate
founder_insight: Ghana is West Africa's most credible inference colocation market outside Nigeria — Equinix AC1, Digital Realty ACR2, and the incoming ADC campus give founders three carrier-neutral PoPs with real cloud on-ramp optionality. The AWS Wavelength Zone (MTN/Orange 5G) is live but limited to edge compute, not full region services; inference workloads needing persistent GPU must route to Paris or Cape Town. The acute risk is power: while DCs are self-sufficient, national grid instability increases OpEx through generator fuel costs.
ai_data_governance_posture: Data Protection Act 2012 in force; sectoral DPA review underway; no data localisation law but cross-border transfer requires DPC authorisation
ai_compute_policy_commitment: National AI Strategy 2023-2033 adopted; Emerging Technologies Bill in Cabinet pipeline; Ministry of Communication actively leading
cross_border_ai_alignment: ECOWAS supplementary data protection act signatory; GDPR adequacy not established; international data flows permitted with DPC authorisation

cloud_providers: Cloudflare (Tunis PoP — unconfirmed, routed via Marseille)
dc_operators: DataXion (Tunis, Tier IV, largest in North Africa)
Poulina Group Holding (Tier IV under construction via APL)
key_regulations: Organic Law 63-2004 on Personal Data Protection (existing)
Draft Fundamental Bill on Personal Data Protection 2025 (GDPR upgrade, pending)
dc_pipeline: SoleCrypt 20MW solar AI DC (Bizerte, 2025/2026 target); Poulina Tier IV DC (under construction); Medusa cable adding 20 Tbps to Marseille (2026); ELMED Tunisia–Italy power/data interconnector (2028)
ai_compute_availability: Growing — DataXion Tier IV and Poulina Tier IV support high-density racks; SoleCrypt GPU-optimized DC (20MW) specifically designed for AI training/inference; sub-50ms latency to Marseille via direct submarine fiber makes Tunisia viable for Europe-serving AI inference
cloud_maturity: Edge PoP only (traffic routes via Marseille/Milan); no hyperscaler region in-country; AWS/Azure/GCP accessible at <50ms via PEACE and Medusa cables
connectivity_role: Mediterranean crossroads — 6 cables connecting to Europe (Marseille, Sicily), Middle East (via PEACE/EIG), and onward to Asia; Bizerte emerging as North Africa's primary cable landing hub; sub-10ms latency claim to Europe possible via direct fiber
power_reliability: Moderate — STEG grid stable for urban/industrial zones; 98% fossil gas dependency creates energy sovereignty risk; grid interconnected with Algeria/Libya for redundancy; renewables <2% of mix but 1,700MW tender pipeline active; DC operators rely on UPS/generators
data_residency_constraint: Existing Organic Law 63-2004 regulates data; draft 2025 bill will impose GDPR-equivalent cross-border transfer rules; national AI strategy promotes data sovereignty; GovCloud initiative reduces reliance on foreign cloud for state data
est_rtt_to_europe_ms: ~30–60ms (Bizerte to Marseille direct via Medusa/PEACE; ~30ms fiber latency cited by SoleCrypt partnership documents)
founder_insight: Tunisia is the most connectivity-advantaged AI inference location in Africa for serving European markets — direct fiber cables to Marseille yield <50ms RTT, matching many Eastern European cloud regions. The SoleCrypt–Schneider Electric partnership signals imminent GPU-capable infrastructure, and InstaDeep's $682M exit has seeded 120+ AI startups. The blocking risks are energy (98% gas-dependent grid) and regulatory uncertainty while the new DP law is still in draft — enterprises should architect for GDPR compliance now to be ahead of the 2025/2026 enactment.
ai_data_governance_posture: National AI Strategy 2021–2025 operational; data protection modernisation bill 2025 aligns with GDPR and Convention 108+; ATI manages TunIXP and national internet infrastructure with sovereignty mandate
ai_compute_policy_commitment: AI Innovation Hub with NVIDIA (Nov 2024); SoleCrypt partnership explicitly targets Tunisia as sustainable AI compute platform for Europe/MENA; Startup Act provides tax incentives for AI companies
cross_border_ai_alignment: Strong EU alignment — Medusa/PEACE cables physically connect to France; draft DP law modeled on GDPR; Startup Act enables easy company formation for EU-facing AI businesses

dc_operators: Government data center (Oran AI DC under construction)
key_regulations: AI data center construction launched March 2025
dc_pipeline: Government AI data center under construction in Oran (March 2025); Huawei government DC agreement
ai_compute_availability: Limited local GPU infrastructure; state-controlled telecom environment restricts foreign cloud; AI DC planned for researchers/startups
cloud_maturity: Developing - local hosting providers active; no hyperscaler regions; government investing in sovereign AI infrastructure
connectivity_role: North Africa cable hub - 5+ Mediterranean cables; excellent latency to Europe; regional gateway
power_reliability: Moderate - 99%+ urban electrification; summer peak demand challenges; 15% renewable target by 2030; grid stress from climate change
data_residency_constraint: Strict data localization requirements; government preference for sovereign cloud; foreign investment restricted
founder_insight: Government AI strategy and Oran data center signal serious intent. State control creates friction for foreign founders but local players benefit from protectionism. Excellent connectivity to Europe enables hybrid approaches.
ai_data_governance_posture: Strong - Law 18-07 established; ANPDP authority active; cybersecurity framework 2025-2030
ai_compute_policy_commitment: Explicit - AI data center under construction; Digital Algeria 2030 includes AI infrastructure
cross_border_ai_alignment: AU Continental AI Strategy; Arab League frameworks; Mediterranean digital partnerships

cloud_providers: AWS Wavelength Zone (Dakar, via Sonatel/Orange, launched April 2025)
key_regulations: Personal Data Protection Law (Loi n 2008-12) — CDP oversees
Personal Data Protection Bill 2019 (AI/cloud — pending full enactment)
dc_pipeline: PAIX Dakar (1.2MW phase 1, operational 2026); Diamniadio campus expansion; Africa Data Centres Senegal site
ai_compute_availability: Very limited — no commercially GPU-capable rack tenancy; AWS Wavelength Zone (2025) for edge compute only; full AI inference requires routing to AWS Paris
cloud_maturity: Wavelength Zone (AWS via Sonatel 5G, April 2025) — most advanced cloud-edge in Francophone West Africa; parent region is Paris
connectivity_role: West Africa coastal hub — 5 submarine cables; historic West Africa connectivity anchor
power_reliability: Moderate — chronic production gap; Taiba N'Diaye wind farm (158MW); ~30% renewable energy mix; major DCs on generator backup
data_residency_constraint: CDP active; current law (2008) being updated; no hard localisation mandate but CDP authorisation required for international transfers
founder_insight: Senegal is the most connectivity-advantaged Francophone West African market for AI inference — 5 submarine cables in Dakar, an AWS Wavelength Zone live since 2025 (first in West Africa), and ~70ms RTT to Paris make it an unusually strong edge-compute candidate relative to its GDP. The gap is GPU colocation: no facility currently meets AI-capable thresholds, and the incoming PAIX Dakar (2026) will be the market's first credible carrier-neutral option. For Francophone AI products, Senegal's policy clarity (SNIA 2023) and routing proximity to Paris make it the natural West Africa anchor once PAIX comes online.
ai_data_governance_posture: CDP active and issuing enforcement notices; 2008 data protection law supplemented by sectoral guidance; updated bill covering AI/biometrics still in legislative pipeline
ai_compute_policy_commitment: National AI Strategy (SNIA 2023) under Ministry of Communication; 'Responsible AI' philosophy; no AI-specific legislation yet
cross_border_ai_alignment: ECOWAS supplementary data protection act signatory; CDP modelled on GDPR principles; Data Privacy Africa network participant

cloud_providers: Angola Cables AngoNAP (carrier-neutral, DE-CIX wholesale reseller)
dc_operators: Raxio Angola AO1 (Luanda, Tier III, 3MW, 800 racks, 2N power, opened Oct 2025)
Angola Cables AngoNAP Luanda (carrier-neutral, ANGONIX IXP host)
key_regulations: Law No. 22/11 on Personal Data Protection (2011); revised draft under consultation 2025
LBTIC 2023-2027 ICT White Paper (approved Dec 2024) — AI priority
dc_pipeline: National Data Center (336 racks, govt, H1 2026); Raxio AO1 operational Oct 2025
ai_compute_availability: Raxio AO1 (3MW, 800 racks, 2N power, 72h fuel backup, 70% renewable); AngoNAP carrier-neutral — both AI-capable
cloud_maturity: Edge PoP only — no hyperscaler region; Angola Cables provides transit to Europe/Brazil/US; DE-CIX partnership improves peering
connectivity_role: Atlantic crossroads — WACS, SACS, MONET, 2Africa; ANGONIX is 3rd largest IXP in Africa by traffic
power_reliability: Frequent brownouts in Luanda (~4-6h/day); national average worse; three separate grid systems; major DCs on on-site generation
data_residency_constraint: APD enforces cross-border transfer restrictions with active fine enforcement; revised law under consultation 2025
founder_insight: Angola's connectivity story is structurally underrated — WACS, SACS, MONET, and ANGONIX (3rd largest African IXP) give Luanda genuine Atlantic crossroads status. Raxio AO1 (Oct 2025) is the country's first AI-capable neutral DC, making Luanda a viable edge inference location for SADC and Lusophone markets. The blocking factors remain power grid reliability outside Luanda and an AI strategy still in pre-adoption drafting phase.
ai_data_governance_posture: Data Protection Law (2011) enforced by APD with active fines; revised law in 2025 consultation; cybersecurity law pending
ai_compute_policy_commitment: LBTIC 2023-2027 explicitly prioritizes AI, IoT, big data; UNESCO AI readiness assessment launched; no standalone national AI strategy adopted yet
cross_border_ai_alignment: SADC member; APD engaged in African data authority networks; DE-CIX partnership enables international peering

submarine_cables: No direct landing — landlocked; connectivity via Uganda/Kenya/Tanzania/DRC to EASSy/SEACOM/2Africa
cloud_providers: PAIX Kigali (cloud-neutral exchange within Kigali Innovation City)
key_regulations: Personal data localisation default — transfer requires DPO certificate
dc_pipeline: Kigali Innovation City full campus ($300M, Africa50/RDB, groundbreaking Sept 2024); PAIX expanding within KIC
ai_compute_availability: Minimal — ADC KGL1 (2.5MW) primary carrier-neutral facility; no confirmed GPU rack tenants; high-density AI workloads require routing to Nairobi
cloud_maturity: Edge PoP only — PAIX Kigali provides cloud on-ramps; tethered to Nairobi for AWS/GCP/Azure
connectivity_role: Landlocked East/Central Africa hub — 99% 4G population coverage
power_reliability: Good by regional standards — hydro 49%, methane gas 38%; major DCs have independent power
data_residency_constraint: DPPL requires local storage by default; cross-border transfer requires NCSA DPO adequacy certificate
founder_insight: Rwanda punches well above its weight for AI founders — it has the continent's clearest AI policy framework (Cabinet-adopted 2023), a functioning data protection authority, pro-business regulation, and the $300M Kigali Innovation City campus coming online. The binding constraint is infrastructure scale: 3 DCs, 1 AI-capable, and no hyperscaler region means all production inference routes through Nairobi. For founders building for Central/East Africa with governance-sensitive use cases (health AI, fintech, government), Rwanda's policy clarity and low ops friction make it the best base-of-operations even if compute must be provisioned remotely.
ai_data_governance_posture: One of Africa's most mature — DPPL in force, DPO actively issuing guidance; National Data Sharing Policy 2025 promotes government data openness
ai_compute_policy_commitment: National AI Policy (April 2023); Kigali Innovation City as physical AI/tech anchor; Rwanda Innovation Fund investing in tech-enabled businesses
cross_border_ai_alignment: EAC data harmonisation participant; DPPL adequacy regime in place; closest in East Africa to GDPR alignment

cloud_providers: Cloud traffic routes via Nairobi or Johannesburg
key_regulations: Data localisation: personal data must remain in Ethiopia
dc_pipeline: Raxio ET1 Phase 2 (IFC $100M funded); Wingu Africa full 10MW buildout
ai_compute_availability: Very scarce — Raxio ET1 is the sole carrier-neutral Tier III facility meeting AI-capable criteria
cloud_maturity: No region, no local zone — regional-tethered to Nairobi or Johannesburg
connectivity_role: Landlocked — dependent on Djibouti for submarine cable access
power_reliability: Moderate — ~100% renewable grid (93% hydro) but 39 outages/month averaging 21h downtime nationally
data_residency_constraint: Personal Data Protection Proclamation 2024 mandates local storage; cross-border transfer requires adequacy determination from ECA
founder_insight: Ethiopia is the largest untapped inference market in Africa by population (126M) and has surprisingly strong policy foundations — both a Data Protection Proclamation (2024) and National AI Policy (2024) are live. The bottleneck is purely infrastructure: landlocked, dependent on Djibouti for all submarine cable access, and only one AI-capable carrier-neutral DC (Raxio ET1). Any production inference deployment must currently use Nairobi or Johannesburg as the anchor region. Data localisation law creates compliance complexity for cross-border model training.
ai_data_governance_posture: Ethiopia's Personal Data Protection Proclamation 2024 is one of sub-Saharan Africa's most comprehensive — includes right to challenge AI-only decisions; localisation-first posture
ai_compute_policy_commitment: National AI Policy (June 2024) designates Ethiopian Artificial Intelligence Institute (EAII) as coordinating body; sectoral AI integration mandated
cross_border_ai_alignment: Localisation-first; adequacy regime under ECA; not yet aligned with GDPR; international model training of Ethiopian data faces legal friction

dc_operators: Paratus Namibia (Armada DC - Tier III, carrier-neutral)
dc_pipeline: Zadara planning Namibia edge node; government digital infrastructure investments
ai_compute_availability: Paratus Armada offers Tier III colocation; no local GPU-as-a-service; edge PoP only for hyperscalers
cloud_maturity: Developing - Paratus Tier III facility operational; highest internet penetration in sub-Saharan Africa outside SA
connectivity_role: Southern Africa cable landing hub; Equiano and ACE landings; regional transit to landlocked neighbors
power_reliability: Moderate - 99.8% transmission availability (NamPower 2024); 45-60% imported from South Africa; improving with renewables (70% target by 2030)
data_residency_constraint: Data Protection Act 2024 requires local processing for sensitive data; strong sovereignty focus
founder_insight: Paratus Armada provides solid foundation for local inference but GPU availability limited. Small market size challenges scalability. Strong governance and stable environment attractive for regional hub strategy.
ai_data_governance_posture: Strong - Data Protection Act 2024 with established authority; cybersecurity index improved to 88/100
ai_compute_policy_commitment: No explicit AI compute policy; Digital Government Strategic Roadmap includes infrastructure
cross_border_ai_alignment: SADC member; AU Continental AI Strategy ratified; aligned with regional frameworks

cloud_providers: Cloud traffic routes via Nairobi or Johannesburg
dc_operators: Vodacom Business DC (12MW, Dar es Salaam)
Raxio Tanzania (under construction, 6MW Tier III, due 2026)
key_regulations: Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) — Data Protection Commission inaugurated 2024
dc_pipeline: Raxio Tanzania (Dar es Salaam, 6MW Tier III, expected 2026); DARE1 cable extension landing (2028)
ai_compute_availability: Very limited — Vodacom 12MW DC not carrier-neutral; Raxio Tanzania not yet operational; no GPU colocation currently available
cloud_maturity: Edge PoP only — tethered to Nairobi or Johannesburg; 2Africa activation 2025 improved backhaul
connectivity_role: East Africa coastal — 6 distributed IXPs nationally
power_reliability: Moderate-low — hydro dependence (drought risk), aging distribution; major DCs use generators
data_residency_constraint: Data Protection Commission active since 2024; cross-border transfers require authorisation; localisation posture evolving
founder_insight: Tanzania is an underrated East African market — 68M population, 6 distributed IXPs, strong mobile penetration, and the incoming Raxio Tier III facility gives it better bones than its current score suggests. The 2Africa cable activation in 2025 materially improved backhaul to European PoPs. The critical blocker today is the complete absence of GPU-capable colocation: all AI inference must route to Nairobi or Johannesburg. When Raxio Tanzania opens in 2026, it will become the most compelling green-field AI colocation site in East Africa below Kenya.
ai_data_governance_posture: Data Protection Commission established 2024; AI Ethical Use Guidelines in 2025 draft; formal law not yet enacted
ai_compute_policy_commitment: Ministry of ICT published draft national AI strategy; first National AI Forum held July 2025
cross_border_ai_alignment: NADPA member (2025); EAC data harmonisation discussions ongoing

cloud_providers: AWS CloudFront (via Djibouti PoP or Nairobi)
dc_operators: Djibouti Data Center / Djibouti Telecom (DDC, carrier-neutral)
Wingu Africa / TO7 Technology Park (Tier III, carrier-neutral, inaugurated Nov 2024)
key_regulations: Digital Code 2025 (enacted June 2025 — e-commerce, data protection, cybercrime, electronic communications)
No standalone AI law — developing via AU Continental AI Strategy participation
dc_pipeline: TO7 Technology Park (Wingu, Tier III, carrier-neutral CLS + DC — inaugurated Nov 2024); AMS-IX Djibouti exchange expansion; Raman cable (Google/Sparkle/OmanTel, landing planned — 2 Africa landings in Egypt and Djibouti)
ai_compute_availability: Limited but strategically positioned — DDC and TO7/Wingu offer carrier-neutral colocation at the world's densest submarine cable crossroads; GPU colocation not marketed but AMS-IX peering and 10 cables enable ultra-low latency routing to Europe/Asia; Cloudflare PoP present; primary value is cable transit and low-latency edge, not local compute
cloud_maturity: Edge PoP (Cloudflare Djibouti; AMS-IX Djibouti peering); hyperscaler full regions absent; Nairobi (AWS/Azure/GCP) reachable at ~20-40ms
connectivity_role: East Africa's premier submarine cable hub — 10+ cables connecting Europe, Middle East, South Asia, and East Africa; DDC and TO7 are the terrestrial junction points; AMS-IX Djibouti (launched Sept 2024) provides neutral peering; strategic importance for Horn of Africa and Red Sea routing; Houthi cable damage incidents (Feb 2024) highlighted geopolitical concentration risk
power_reliability: Moderate risk — national grid has reliability issues (summer peaks, theft incidents); data centers (DDC, TO7) have independent power infrastructure; 58.9MW Ghoubet Wind Farm (2023) first utility-scale renewable; government targets 100% renewable by 2035; urban commercial zones better served than residential
data_residency_constraint: Digital Code 2025 introduces comprehensive data protection regime (156 articles, enacted June 2025); cross-border transfer rules now codified; Djibouti Telecom monopoly on international cables creates de-facto routing transparency risk for sensitive data
est_rtt_to_europe_ms: ~80–110ms (via EIG/AAE-1 direct to Europe; ~80ms to Marseille measured; Red Sea routing adds geopolitical risk post-Houthi incidents)
founder_insight: Djibouti's unique value for AI infrastructure is as a cable transit hub, not a compute destination — 10 submarine cables, AMS-IX peering, and Cloudflare PoP make it the lowest-latency East African entry point for European and Middle Eastern cloud traffic. The TO7/Wingu Tier III DC (Nov 2024) and DDC offer carrier-neutral colocation that is genuinely differentiated for network-intensive AI workloads like inference routing and caching. The critical risks are Djibouti Telecom's monopoly control of cable infrastructure, the Feb 2024 Houthi cable damage events proving concentration vulnerability, and a startup ecosystem that remains pre-commercial. Founders building East Africa–Europe latency-sensitive AI products should consider Djibouti as a network PoP, not a primary compute base.
ai_data_governance_posture: Digital Code 2025 establishes first comprehensive data protection framework; cybersecurity strategy 2024–2030 active; state monopoly (Djibouti Telecom) over cables creates single point of control; MENI (Ministry of Digital Economy) leads digital governance
ai_compute_policy_commitment: Smart Nation strategy includes 150 digital projects ($850M); TO7 Technology Park Innovation Center to nurture tech talent; government participated in AU Continental AI Strategy (July 2024); no dedicated GPU investment programme
cross_border_ai_alignment: Digital Code 2025 aligns with international norms; AU Continental AI Strategy member; cable infrastructure serves as de-facto Africa–Europe–Asia AI data highway; EU and Gulf state investment presence growing

dc_pipeline: ST Digital Tier III facility completion expected 2025; government digital hub investments
ai_compute_availability: ADC Lomé provides carrier-neutral Tier III; ST Digital will add capacity; no local GPU-as-a-service; Equiano cable improves latency
cloud_maturity: Developing - first carrier-neutral DC operational 2021; ST Digital expansion; West Africa digital hub ambitions
connectivity_role: West Africa cable landing hub - 3 submarine cables including Equiano; regional transit to Ghana/Benin/Burkina Faso; TGIX IXP operational
power_reliability: Poor - 50% imported from Ghana/Nigeria; high costs ($0.25/kWh); 41% renewable (2024) with 50% target by 2030; tight supply
data_residency_constraint: Data Protection Law 2019 requires authorization for cross-border transfers; Digital 2025 strategy emphasizes sovereignty
founder_insight: Small but dynamic market with strong government digital focus. Equiano cable provides competitive connectivity. ST Digital facility will strengthen regional position. Good testbed for Francophone West Africa solutions.
ai_data_governance_posture: Strong - Data Protection Law 2019 with established authority; Digital 2025 strategy; cybersecurity index 88/100
ai_compute_policy_commitment: Implicit via Digital 2025; AI and Big Data identified as priority sectors
cross_border_ai_alignment: ECOWAS member; AU Continental AI Strategy; Smart Africa initiative participant

dc_operators: Botswana Telecommunications Corporation (BTC Sentlhaga, Tier II)
key_regulations: Data Protection Act 18 of 2024 (in force Jan 2025)
dc_pipeline: Digital Delta DC1 (BoFiNet, Tier III, 400 racks, launched Oct 2025); additional phases planned at Botswana Innovation Hub
ai_compute_availability: Limited — 1 Tier III carrier-neutral DC with cloud on-ramp; GPU colocation not yet marketed; inference workloads must route via Johannesburg or Cape Town
cloud_maturity: Edge PoP only (via Johannesburg)
connectivity_role: Landlocked transit consumer; relies on South Africa and Namibia gateways for all international traffic; Paratus BKF terrestrial route to Equiano cable (Swakopmund) provides a western corridor
power_reliability: Moderate risk — coal-dependent national grid with periodic load shedding; DCs rely on generators; 335MW renewable pipeline (solar/wind) under development; World Bank RESA project approved July 2024
data_residency_constraint: Data Protection Act 2024 imposes cross-border transfer restrictions requiring adequate protection; IDPC enforcement body active from 2025
est_rtt_to_europe_ms: ~130–150ms (via Paratus BKF/Equiano to Lisbon; ~123ms measured on express route)
founder_insight: Botswana's new Tier III Digital Delta DC (BoFiNet) and the Paratus BKF–Equiano express route to Europe create a credible on-ramp for cloud and inference workloads for the first time, but GPU colocation is not yet available locally — founders will route AI inference via Johannesburg or use cloud edge. The Data Protection Act 2024 adds compliance overhead but also signals long-term regulatory maturity. With Techstars entering and the Botswana Innovation Hub active, early-stage AI companies can find community infrastructure, though Series A capital remains scarce.
ai_data_governance_posture: GDPR-aligned Data Protection Act 2024 with IDPC oversight; cross-border transfer controls active; AI-specific regulation not yet drafted
ai_compute_policy_commitment: Government explicitly positions Digital Delta DC as AI and cloud enabler; ICT Roadmap 2030 targets knowledge-based economy; no dedicated AI compute fund yet
cross_border_ai_alignment: Aligned with AU Data Policy Framework; Data Protection Act modeled on GDPR; SADC digital integration in progress

dc_operators: CAMTEL Zamengoe DC (Yaoundé, Tier III, 3000sqm, carrier-neutral, SAT-3/WACS direct access)
ST Digital Douala (commercial colocation and cloud)
key_regulations: Comprehensive Data Protection Law (adopted Dec 23, 2024) — DPA being established, 18-month compliance window
National AI Strategy referenced but not formally adopted
dc_pipeline: No major confirmed pipeline as of early 2026
ai_compute_availability: CAMTEL Zamengoe is Tier III but not GPU/AI-workload marketed; no hyperscaler edge PoP or GPU compute in-country
cloud_maturity: Very low — no hyperscaler region or Local Zone; traffic routes via Lagos or European hubs
connectivity_role: 4 submarine cable landings (SAT-3, WACS, ACE, NCSCS at Kribi/Douala); CAMIX IXP with 3 PoPs
power_reliability: Poor — 6-8h outages daily since late Dec 2024; Eneo rationing; Nachtigal dam (+300MW) provides partial relief
data_residency_constraint: New DPL (Dec 2024) establishes DPA; cross-border transfer rules operationalizing through 2026; compliance burden timing uncertain
founder_insight: Cameroon has surprisingly strong raw connectivity — four submarine cable landings and a Tier III carrier-neutral DC (CAMTEL Zamengoe) make it structurally better connected than most Central African peers. The critical blockers are severe power unreliability (6-8h cuts daily) and the complete absence of hyperscaler cloud on-ramps, meaning all AI inference must route through Lagos or Europe. The December 2024 data protection law is a genuine positive signal, though its 18-month compliance window means the regulatory landscape is still fluid.
ai_data_governance_posture: First comprehensive DPL adopted Dec 2024; DPA being established; AI governance lags significantly
ai_compute_policy_commitment: National AI Strategy referenced but not formally adopted or budgeted; Camtel-University AI Training Centre operational
cross_border_ai_alignment: CEMAC member; new DPL designed to align with international standards; no AI-specific cross-border data governance framework

key_regulations: Personal Data Protection Act amended 2023 (Law 025/2023) - includes AI definitions
National Technical Committee for AI (CTN-IA) established December 2023
dc_pipeline: Cybastion government DC under construction; ST Digital operational since 2025
ai_compute_availability: ST Digital Tier III facility offers sovereign cloud; no GPU-as-a-service yet; oil wealth enables infrastructure investment
cloud_maturity: Developing - first carrier-neutral Tier III operational 2025; government focus on digital sovereignty
connectivity_role: Central Africa cable hub - 4 submarine cables; 2Africa landing improves capacity; GABIX IXP operational
power_reliability: Moderate - better than regional peers due to oil wealth; demand exceeds supply (700MW demand vs 500MW capacity); aging infrastructure causing outages; emergency plan 2025-2028
data_residency_constraint: 2023 Data Protection amendment includes AI provisions; APDPVP can impose fines up to CFA 100M; sovereign cloud preference
founder_insight: ST Digital facility is game-changer for Central Africa. Oil wealth provides infrastructure funding but small market size limits scalability. First-mover advantage for founders establishing regional presence.
ai_data_governance_posture: Improving - 2023 amendment explicitly addresses AI; CTN-IA conducting readiness assessment
ai_compute_policy_commitment: Implicit via Digital 2025 strategy; sovereign cloud emphasis
cross_border_ai_alignment: CEMAC member; AU Continental AI Strategy; UNECA sub-regional AI workshop participant

cloud_providers: No AWS/Azure/GCP region or Local Zone
dc_operators: Raxio Uganda UG1 (Namanve, Tier III, 400 racks, 1.4MW, carrier-neutral, operational 2021)
dc_pipeline: NITA-U National IP Peering Exchange (NIPX) announced Oct 2025; Raxio IFC $100M funding (Apr 2025) may enable UG2
ai_compute_availability: Raxio UG1 is the only carrier-neutral Tier III DC; 400 racks at full scale; no GPU cloud PoPs confirmed
cloud_maturity: Very low — landlocked dependency on Kenyan fiber; hyperscaler access routes via SEACOM/EASSy to South Africa
connectivity_role: Landlocked — all international capacity transits cross-border fiber to Mombasa or Dar es Salaam; UIXP has 32 networks (~40Gbps peak)
power_reliability: Unreliable — ~4h average outages/day; commercial DCs on diesel generator backup
data_residency_constraint: DPPA 2019 restricts personal data transfer to countries without adequate protection; PDPO enforcement active
founder_insight: Uganda has East Africa's most mature neutral DC outside Kenya and Ethiopia (Raxio UG1, Tier III since 2021) and a compelling demographic tailwind — 73% of population under 30 with cloud adoption growing 30%+ per year. The structural constraint is landlocked connectivity: all international traffic traverses expensive cross-border fiber to Mombasa or Dar es Salaam, creating a cost floor 2-3x higher than coastal peers. The AI policy is now formally being drafted, meaning founders face 12-18 months of regulatory uncertainty before the framework is settled.
ai_data_governance_posture: DPPA 2019 is among East Africa's more mature data protection laws with an active PDPO enforcement office; AI governance formally initiated 2024
ai_compute_policy_commitment: No formal AI compute investment commitment; IFC-backed Raxio expansion and NITA-U NIPX announcements signal intent
cross_border_ai_alignment: EAC digital integration framework member; PDPO engaged with African data protection authorities

key_regulations: National AI and Big Data Strategy (SNIAM 2023-2027)
dc_pipeline: No major AI-capable DC investments announced; government digital infrastructure focus
ai_compute_availability: No local GPU infrastructure; dependent on Nigeria (Lagos) or Ghana cloud regions; proximity to Lagos hub beneficial
cloud_maturity: Early - government DC exists but not AI-capable; no carrier-neutral Tier III facilities
connectivity_role: West Africa cable landing (ACE, SAT-3); regional transit to Niger/Burkina Faso; BENIN-IX operational
power_reliability: Poor - 28 interruptions/month average; 75% electricity imported from Nigeria/Ghana; high costs ($0.25/kWh); improving with EBRD investment
data_residency_constraint: Data Protection Law restricts cross-border transfers; limited local capacity for compliance
founder_insight: SNIAM 2027 strategy shows ambition but infrastructure lags. Proximity to Lagos enables reliance on Nigerian cloud. Government digital services creating demand for local hosting.
ai_data_governance_posture: Strong - Comprehensive SNIAM strategy; Digital Code foundation; APDP operational
ai_compute_policy_commitment: SNIAM includes AI infrastructure development; controlled environment for AI initiatives planned
cross_border_ai_alignment: ECOWAS member; AU Continental AI Strategy; WAEMU regulatory frameworks

cloud_providers: Azure Stack Hub (Zambia cited as supported market)
dc_operators: Paratus Zambia Vanguard DC (Lusaka, Tier III designed, ISO-certified, carrier-neutral, operational)
key_regulations: Data Protection Act 2021 (Office of Data Protection Commissioner)
dc_pipeline: Lusaka South MFEZ Tier III DC (planned construction 2024, status uncertain as of 2026)
ai_compute_availability: Paratus Vanguard DC provides Tier III colocation; no GPU compute or AI-marketed workloads; Paratus metro fiber (Meta partnership) completed 2024
cloud_maturity: Very low — doubly landlocked from submarine cables; Azure Stack cited but no confirmed regional infrastructure
connectivity_role: Doubly landlocked — accesses SEACOM/EASSy via Zimbabwe or Tanzania terrestrial routes; Lusaka IXP (LIXP) has 12 members
power_reliability: Severe — 8-12h daily load shedding throughout 2024-2025; Kariba Dam drought reduced hydropower output
data_residency_constraint: DPA 2021 requires consent for cross-border transfers and Data Protection Commissioner approval; enforcement currently light
founder_insight: Zambia holds the most complete policy stack — both an enacted Data Protection Act (2021) and adopted National AI Strategy (2024-2026) — creating regulatory clarity that is rare in Sub-Saharan Africa. However, infrastructure reality completely disconnects from policy ambition: catastrophic load shedding (8-12h daily driven by Kariba Dam drought) and double-landlocked connectivity make reliable AI inference commercially impractical today. Zambia is a medium-term watch market, not a near-term deployment target.
ai_data_governance_posture: DPA 2021 operational with active Data Protection Commissioner; National AI Strategy 2024-2026 adopted — strongest combined policy posture despite weakest infrastructure
ai_compute_policy_commitment: National AI Strategy 2024-2026 has dedicated infrastructure pillar and proposes National AI Council; ZICTA working on AI governance frameworks
cross_border_ai_alignment: SADC member; DPA 2021 aligned with international standards; AI Strategy mentions cross-border governance but implementation remains early

dc_operators: Raxio Mozambique (MZ1, Tier III, 3MW, 400 racks — Beluluane)
key_regulations: Draft Personal Data Protection Law (circulated 2024, not yet enacted)
dc_pipeline: Raxio MZ1 expansion (Beluluane, additional MW capacity); iColo MPM1 2Africa cable landing station expansion; MOZIX expansion into MPM1 (completed May 2025); Internet for All project (govt, targets 100% coverage by 2030)
ai_compute_availability: Very limited — Raxio MZ1 is the only Tier III carrier-neutral DC capable of supporting moderate AI workloads; 3MW capacity and 2Africa/SEACOM/EASSy connectivity gives it potential, but no GPU colocation marketed; all serious AI inference routes via Johannesburg
cloud_maturity: Edge PoP only (Cloudflare Maputo PoP; all hyperscaler full regions in Johannesburg)
connectivity_role: Emerging East African transit point — 3 submarine cables (SEACOM, EASSy, 2Africa) land at Maputo and Nacala; Raxio MZ1 positioned as domestic interconnection hub; 2Africa Nacala landing opens northern corridor; capacity significantly underutilised
power_reliability: Poor — frequent grid outages; Maputo suffers load shedding; 2024 election protests caused deliberate infrastructure disruption; EDM grid ~60% electrification nationally; DCs require full generator backup; hydropower dependency creates seasonal vulnerability
data_residency_constraint: No enacted data protection law; draft law circulated 2024 but timeline unclear; absence of legal framework creates compliance ambiguity for international AI deployments; operators currently self-regulate
est_rtt_to_europe_ms: ~160–200ms (via South Africa then submarine cable to Europe)
founder_insight: Mozambique is a frontier market where infrastructure fundamentals are only now being established — Raxio MZ1 is the country's first credible carrier-neutral DC and 2Africa's Nacala landing adds a second cable corridor, but the absence of a data protection law, frequent power outages, and lack of GPU colocation mean AI inference must route through Johannesburg for the foreseeable future. The opportunity is in serving Mozambique's 34M population with mobile-first AI applications via the Cloudflare Maputo PoP, not in running inference locally. Founders should treat MZ as a market-entry priority rather than an infrastructure base.
ai_data_governance_posture: No enacted data protection law; draft circulating since 2024; UNESCO AI readiness assessment completed but no policy action yet; Electronic Transactions Law 2017 provides minimal framework
ai_compute_policy_commitment: Government Digital Transformation Strategy acknowledges AI potential; Internet for All project shows intent; no dedicated AI compute investment or GPU incentive programme
cross_border_ai_alignment: Minimal — not yet party to AU Data Policy Framework implementation; draft DP law may align with AU standards when enacted; SADC digital integration aspirational

ai_compute_availability: GPU access limited to Raxio DRC1; no hyperscaler regions; expensive international cloud

connectivity_role: Regional transit hub for Central Africa; ACE cable landing at Muanda
power_reliability: Catastrophic nationally - ~12 outages/month; Kinshasa slightly better with generators; Inga dam underutilized
data_residency_constraint: Government preference for local hosting per Digital 2025 plan; limited enforcement capacity
founder_insight: Raxio DRC1 provides first viable local AI inference option but power costs and reliability remain major barriers. Founders should plan for hybrid architectures with significant edge caching.
ai_data_governance_posture: Basic - Digital Code 2023 provides foundation but no DPA operational
ai_compute_policy_commitment: None explicit
cross_border_ai_alignment: AU Continental AI Strategy adopted July 2024; no national implementation

dc_operators: Government Tier III National Data Center (Bamako, inaugurated January 2026)
dc_pipeline: New Tier III facility just inaugurated; AES regional digital sovereignty initiative
ai_compute_availability: Government DC provides sovereign hosting; no GPU-specific infrastructure; designed for government data repatriation

connectivity_role: Landlocked - dependent on Senegal/Ivory Coast terrestrial; Alliance of Sahel States (AES) digital sovereignty focus; MLIX IXP operational

data_residency_constraint: AES digital sovereignty priority - data repatriation from foreign servers; government platforms must use local DC
founder_insight: New Tier III DC is major milestone but political instability and power crisis limit viability. AES sovereignty agenda creates opportunity for regional hosting but security concerns deter international investment.
ai_data_governance_posture: Basic - Cybersecurity strategy approved 2025; data protection law under development; military government limits international alignment
ai_compute_policy_commitment: None explicit; focus on government data sovereignty rather than AI compute
cross_border_ai_alignment: AES (Alliance of Sahel States) coordination with Burkina Faso/Niger; limited AU engagement due to sanctions

dc_operators: Sudatel (partially operational in Port Sudan area only)
key_regulations: Cybercrime and Computer Misuse Order (under review)
dc_pipeline: No viable pipeline - civil war has suspended infrastructure development
ai_compute_availability: No local GPU infrastructure; data centers captured or destroyed in Khartoum; ISPs relocated to Port Sudan with limited capacity
cloud_maturity: Collapsed - civil war destroyed most digital infrastructure; internet shutdowns widespread; 9M+ IDPs; GDP dropped 40%+
connectivity_role: Civil war has severed most connectivity; Port Sudan maintains limited cable access; SIXP IXP degraded
power_reliability: Catastrophic - over 70% of Khartoum power infrastructure destroyed; satellite data shows country-wide outages; hospitals and critical services without power
data_residency_constraint: No functional regulatory framework; conflict priorities override digital policy
founder_insight: Civil war has made Sudan uninvestable for AI infrastructure. Founders who remain focus on survival apps (Cashi, Nidaa). Post-war reconstruction may create opportunities but timeline uncertain. Savannah Innovation Labs shows resilience.
ai_data_governance_posture: Non-existent - no functional data protection authority; draft laws pre-war not enacted
ai_compute_policy_commitment: None
cross_border_ai_alignment: Conflict has isolated Sudan from regional frameworks; AU engagement minimal
